In particular, the father’s criminal history, which includes multiple convictions for offences such as driving whilst disqualified and breaches of bonds and bail conditions, suggests a persistent unwillingness or inability on the part of the father to comply with court orders and restrictions. Similarly, the father’s failure to comply with orders such as those requiring him to undertake parenting courses leaves little basis for any expectation that he can be relied upon to comply with binding court orders, particularly any orders that are not to his liking.

The experts’ recommendations

Dr O opined that a cautious approach was warranted, and recommended that the father have supervised time with X, progressing to unsupervised time upon completion of a neuropsychological assessment and information being obtained from Dr H as to his recent progress and compliance with treatment, and that there be a reassessment with a view to progressing to unsupervised time if supervised time progressed well.

Dr P opined that it was inappropriate for the father to be caring for a young child by himself, but that, subject to there being an appropriate supervisor, it may be possible for him to have some level of supervised time, though the guidelines for this would need to be clear and well-defined. He recommended professional supervision and opined that if that were not possible, the father’s involvement with X should be delayed until she was older and more independent. In the witness box, he confirmed that by this he meant it should be delayed until she was 18 and able to make her own choices.

Dr H supported the reintroduction of X to the father by way of regular supervised contact visits.

Ultimately, when balancing all the competing considerations, Ms J recommended that X spend no time with the father. At the conclusion of her evidence, she confirmed that nothing she had heard or read since writing her report had caused her to change that recommendation.

It was submitted on behalf of the father that Ms J’s recommendations were “out of step” with those of the other experts. That may be so, but it is Ms J’s recommendations to which I give the greatest weight. Not only is Ms J the only expert witness who has met or observed X and the only expert witness who has undertaken a holistic assessment of all the relevant considerations including X’s relationships and developmental needs, she is the expert whose particular area of expertise is recommendations as to parenting arrangements.

Furthermore, Dr P’s recommendations must be considered in the context of his evidence that assuming the accuracy of the mother’s assertion that the father was and is an extremely violent person (being an assertion consistent with my findings), exposing a child to such an individual would potentially be an act of cruelty towards the mother and the child.

Conclusion

The evidence does not support a finding that the identified risks to X can sufficiently be ameliorated by any protective measures. I accept the submission made on behalf of the ICL that this is a case in which an order for time would do more harm than good, and it would be in X’s best interests to have no time with the father.

In circumstances in which there is no existing relationship between X and the father, and particularly in light of the evidence as to the likely ongoing impacts on the mother (and indirectly on X) of the involvement of the father in X’s life, I find that it is also not in X’s best interests for there to be communication or any other form of engagement or involvement by the father in her life or that of the mother.

INJUNCTIONS

Each of the parties and the ICL sought a range of injunctions.

The power to grant injunctions is found in subsection 68B(1) of the Act, which provides, in summary, that the Court may make such order or grant such injunction as it considers appropriate for the welfare of the child. Although the paramountcy principle in section 60CA does not apply to the granting of an injunction, X’s best interests are a relevant consideration.[58]

In my view, to the extent that they relate to the same matters to which the family violence order relates, subsection 114AB(2) provides a barrier to the making of many of the injunctions sought on the application of the mother. However, the operation of subsection 114AB does not operate to preclude the application made by the ICL.

The injunctions sought by the ICL are appropriately necessary and protective, having regard to the findings I have made, and are both proportionate and adapted to the identified risks. I am satisfied that they are appropriate for X’s welfare.

The evidence before the Court, particularly the evidence regarding the mother’s appropriate, protective and child-focused parenting, does not support there being any basis for the injunctions sought by the father.

FURTHER PROCEEDINGS

Ms J suggested that the father be at liberty to instigate further proceedings in future once X is older and once he has demonstrated a sustained capacity for change. Such an order was not sought by either party or by the ICL.

In any event, in light of the compelling evidence given by the mother of the harmful impact on her and her mental health of her involvement in the present proceedings, combined with the risk of adverse impacts on her parenting, I do not consider it likely to serve X’s best interests for the Court to encourage or condone further proceedings beyond what is already provided for within the scope of section 65DAAA of the Act.

CONCLUSION

Having regard to all of the considerations as outlined above, I make orders as set out at the commencement of these reasons, which I am satisfied are in X’s best interests.

desc.zh: 作出养育令时，子女最佳利益是首要考量。
desc.en: In deciding whether to make a parenting order, the child's best interests are the paramount consideration.

desc.zh: 列出判断最佳利益须考量的事项，安全（含免受家暴）为聚焦的起点。
desc.en: Lists the matters to consider for best interests, with safety (including from family violence) the focused starting point.

desc.zh: 定义家庭暴力为胁迫、控制或致家庭成员恐惧的暴力/威胁/其他行为，不以实际造成控制或恐惧为必要。
desc.en: Defines family violence as violent, threatening or other behaviour that coerces, controls or causes a family member to be fearful; it need not in fact coerce, control or cause fear.

sc.zh: 为子女福祉作出禁制令

desc.zh: 授权法院作出其认为适当、以保障子女福祉的禁制令;子女最佳利益为相关考量。
desc.en: Empowers the court to grant injunctions it considers appropriate for a child's welfare; the child's best interests are a relevant consideration.

sc.zh: 避免不可接受的家暴风险

desc.zh: 要求法院在切实可行范围内，确保所作命令不致使人面临不可接受的家庭暴力风险。
desc.en: Requires the court, so far as practicable, to ensure its orders do not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence.

sc.zh: 重启终局养育令的途径
sc.en: Avenue to reopen final parenting orders

desc.zh: 为日后在重大情势变更等条件下重新审视终局养育令提供既有途径。
desc.en: Provides the existing avenue to reconsider final parenting orders later, on a significant change of circumstances.

summary.zh: 确立「不可接受风险」检验，为各类风险主张的试金石。
summary.en: Lays down the 'unacceptable risk' test, the touchstone for risk claims of any kind.

prop.zh: 以不可接受风险为养育安全的核心标准。
prop.en: Unacceptable risk is the central standard for safety in parenting.

court.zh: 家事法院全席 Full Court · 2009
court.en: Full Court of the Family Court · 2009

summary.zh: 并非为相处而相处;弊大于利时无须安排相处。
summary.en: Not contact for contact's sake; no time need be ordered where it would do more harm than good.

prop.zh: 相处须对子女有正向益处方予安排。
prop.en: Time is ordered only where it offers positive benefit to the child.

summary.zh: 「不相处」命令应限于子女最佳利益明确要求、监督无法化解风险之情形。
summary.en: No-time orders should be confined to cases plainly required by best interests where supervision cannot ameliorate the risk.

prop.zh: 切断亲子相处须与风险相称、属最后手段。
prop.en: Severing parent-child time must be proportionate to risk and a last resort.

summary.zh: Part VII 将子女与他人的安全置于考量的聚焦起点。
summary.en: Part VII places safety as the focused starting point in the considerations.

prop.zh: 安全是养育裁判的起点。
prop.en: Safety is the starting point in parenting determinations.

summary.zh: 区分证人的可信性（诚实）与可靠性（准确感知、记忆、转述）。
summary.en: Distinguishes a witness's credibility (honesty) from reliability (accurate perception, recall and relay).

prop.zh: 评估证据须分辨可信性与可靠性。
prop.en: Assessing evidence requires distinguishing credibility from reliability.

summary.zh: 越严重的指控，认定所需的确信程度越高。
summary.en: The more serious the allegation, the higher the degree of persuasion required to find it.

prop.zh: 严重指控须有更强的证明确信。
prop.en: Serious allegations demand a stronger degree of actual persuasion.

fulltext:
PARKER J:

INTRODUCTION

Before the Court are the parties’ competing applications for parenting orders with respect to the child X, who was born in in 2020 and is aged five.
The applicant father, MR DAYAN (“the father”), was born in 1977 and is aged 48. The respondent mother, MS SHAUL (“the mother”), was born in 1987 and is aged 38.
The parties shared a very brief relationship, the precise nature of which is not entirely clear from the evidence before the Court, but which appears to have commenced a short time prior to the mother falling pregnant with X and to have ended in or around mid-2020, prior to X’s birth. They did not marry and did not cohabit but spent time together at each other’s homes.
X has lived in the full-time care of the mother since her birth. The father was not notified by the mother of X’s birth and did not meet her for a number of months after her birth. The parties dispute the circumstances in which the father came to meet X, but agree that he met her on only a small number of occasions when she was an infant. He has not seen her since approximately April 2021, when she was an infant. At the time of the trial, X was living with the mother and spending no time with the father pursuant to interim orders made on 19 August 2024.
The mother has a daughter from a previous relationship, B, who was born on in 2014 and is aged 11. The mother lives with B and X in an undisclosed location. She works in hospitality and undertakes part-time study.
The father lives with his partner of approximately four years, MS C, who is also his carer. He receives a disability support pension and works on a part-time basis in manual labour. The father has a son from a previous relationship, MR D, who was born in 2005 and is aged 20. Following the father’s separation from Mr D’s mother, MS E, the father resided with the paternal grandparents. Mr D lived with his mother and spent time with the father at the paternal grandparents’ residence under their supervision.
Ms C has two daughters, MS F, aged 18 and G aged 17. Both daughters grew up in long-term foster placements, and the younger child remains in such a placement. Ms C and the father have a relationship with and spend time with both of Ms C’s daughters.
There has been no direct communication between the parties since approximately April 2021.
The current proceedings were commenced by the father on 24 October 2023, approximately two and a half years after he last saw X. They were transferred to this Court on 31 July 2025.
X’s interests were represented in these proceedings by an Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”).

THE PARTIES’ APPLICATIONS

The parties agreed that X would continue to live with the mother and that the mother would have sole parental responsibility[1] and decision-making responsibility with respect to major long-term issues for her. Those orders are supported by the evidence before the Court and I am satisfied that they are in X’s best interests.
The father sought leave to amend his application twice during the course of the trial. Some but not all of the amendments he proposed were permitted. To the extent that leave was not granted, this was as a consequence of likely prejudice to the mother and the risk that the amendment would necessitate an adjournment of the trial. The father’s application as ultimately amended sought, in summary:
(a) That X spend time with him for eight visits under professional supervision at a supervised contact centre;

(b) That X thereafter spend unsupervised time with him in a gradually increasing regime commencing with daytime only and building over time to alternate weekends from the conclusion of school on Friday until the commencement of school on Monday and special occasions;

(c) As an alternative proposal in the event the Court deemed it necessary, the time following the visits in the contact centre take place substantially in the presence of Ms C;

(d) That X communicate with him by way of video call on two occasions per week;

(e) That he be at liberty to engage in family therapy with X;

(f) Injunctions restraining each of the parties from denigration, publication on social media, consuming illicit substances prior to or during time they have the care of X and discussing the proceedings with X; and

(g) A number of other orders regulating coparenting, including orders regarding attendance at events of significant to X and communication between the parties.

The mother sought a range of injunctions against the father. Although the mother had initially sought a slightly different form of injunctions, at the conclusion of the trial, counsel appearing on her behalf advised that she adopted the proposals of the ICL with respect to injunctions. She also adopted the ICL’s proposal that there be an order providing that X spend no time with the father, her original position having been simply that there should be no order with respect to time.
The ICL proposed:
(a) X spend no time with the father.

(b) Injunctions restraining the father from:

(i) Approaching within 100 metres of X and/or the mother, or any care facility, school, residence, place of employment of X or the mother from time to time;

(ii) Communicating with X and/or the mother;

(iii) Removing or attempting to remove X from the care of the mother or any person to whom the mother has delegated her care, or causing or permitting another person to do so; and

(iv) Publishing any material relating to X or the mother on any social media.

Although the ICL’s outline made reference to an order for a contribution to the costs of the ICL by the father, counsel for the ICL did not press that aspect of the application at the trial.

MATERIAL RELIED ON

The father relied on the following documents:
(a) Amended Initiating Application filed on 8 April 2026 (during the trial);

(b) Trial affidavit filed on 20 February 2026;

(c) Affidavit of Ms C (his partner) filed on 20 February 2026;

(d) Report of Dr H (his treating psychiatrist) dated 8 January 2026;[2] and

(e) Case outline filed on 27 March 2026.

The mother relied on the following documents:
(a) Response to Initiating Application filed 23 March 2024;

(b) Trial affidavit filed 26 February 2026; and

(c) Case outline filed 23 March 2026.

The ICL relied on a case outline filed on 23 March 2026.
The operation of rule 8.15(3)(e) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) was dispensed with insofar as it related to the reports of single experts and other professional witnesses and the reports of such experts were accepted into evidence. Each of the parties and the ICL also tendered documents throughout the course of the hearing. Although each and every aspect of the evidence relied upon by the parties has not been referred to in these reasons, I have had regard to the totality of the evidence before the Court and the submissions of each of the parties.

SINGLE EXPERT REPORTS

The Court was assisted by the following single expert reports:
(a) A family report prepared by Panel Family Consultant Ms J dated 15 April 2025;

(b) A psychiatric assessment of the father prepared by Dr O, psychiatrist, dated 2 May 2024; and

(c) Psychological assessments of each of the parties undertaken by Dr P, psychologist, dated 10 October 2024 (mother) and 14 October 2024 (father).

Dr P and Ms J each gave evidence and were cross-examined by counsel for each of the parties and the ICL. Their opinions were not undermined to any significant degree in cross-examination and there was no challenge to their qualifications or expertise. I accept their evidence as to their observations and give substantial weight to their expert opinions.
Dr O’s report was admitted by consent and he was not required for cross-examination. I accept his evidence in its entirety.

THE PARTIES AND WITNESSES AND THEIR EVIDENCE

In 2006, the father sustained a serious brain injury, which resulted in him spending 14 days in an induced coma, following which he required extensive rehabilitation. The father continues to suffer significant effects from this injury. This, potentially in combination with mental health conditions suffered by him (which are discussed later in these reasons), substantially affected the reliability of the father’s evidence, in the following ways:
(a) The father’s memory, including his ability to recall important events, is very poor. His response to a very large number of the questions he was asked by way of cross-examination was that he could not recall. He was unable to recall important events in the proceedings, such as his meetings with the single experts, and significant events in his life, such as his engagement with the criminal justice system.

(b) The father’s evidence was inconsistent in a number of respects, some of which may not have been deliberate. For example, he deposed that the parties had never had a relationship but readily agreed in the witness box that they had.

(c) The father provided information that was objectively incorrect, but which was plainly not intended to mislead. For example, he incorrectly referred to his 20-year-old son as being aged 19 and to events that occurred during the parties’ relationship as having occurred thirty years prior.

The impressions of the father’s evidence outlined above are not intended to be criticisms of the father. The effects of his injury on his functioning are outside his control and clearly cause considerable difficulty for the father in daily living. It is to be expected that the complexities of court proceedings would add an additional layer of challenge, and I am not critical of the father for his efforts to meet those challenges. It was evident that at least in a large number of instances, the father’s inability to recall facts and events was genuinely relayed by him.
The reliability of the evidence of a witness can be distinguished from the credibility of that witness. In IMM v R[3] Nettle and Gordon JJ of the High Court of Australia (“the High Court”) discussed the distinction as follows:
... it is important to be clear about what is meant by “credibility” and “reliability” in this context. At common law, a distinction was ordinarily drawn between the two concepts. The credibility of a witness was commonly understood as meaning the "truthfulness" of the witness – whether the witness genuinely believed that he or she was telling the truth. Reliability, on the other hand, referred to the ability of the witness accurately to discern and relay the truth as to an event, including the witness's ability to observe and remember facts. For example, if an event occurred a long time ago, that might affect the reliability of the witness because it is generally accepted that memory is prone to fade over time. Credibility and reliability are used in those senses throughout these reasons.

(Footnote omitted)
The issues with the reliability of the father’s evidence which I have outlined render the task of assessment of his credibility difficult. In my view, however, it is not necessary to undertake a global assessment of the father’s credibility as a witness. The limitations to the reliability of his evidence are such that his evidence cannot be accepted in the absence of corroborative evidence, at least to the extent that it was disputed.
The mother gave her evidence in a direct and forthright manner. She became distressed at times when discussing difficult subjects. The mother’s presentation when giving her evidence was consistent with an observation made by Ms J that she provided comprehensive responses and presented as a protective and child-focused parent who was focused on X’s safety. Her evidence was not undermined in cross-examination. To the extent that the evidence of the parties conflicts, I prefer the evidence of the mother.
The father gave his evidence from the witness box physically located in the courtroom. The mother gave hers via videolink from a secure room within the precincts of the Court. Although an order permitting the mother to give evidence from a secure room was made by consent on 24 March 2026 (prior to the commencement of the trial), before she commenced her evidence, an application was made on behalf of the father for her to give her evidence in the courtroom, during which period it was proposed that the father observe from a secure room. Counsel for the father submitted that having the mother give her evidence in the courtroom would allow the parties’ evidence to be assessed “in like terms” in the same context and environment. The application was refused.
In assessing the parties’ evidence, I have had regard to the differences in the manner in which each party’s evidence was given. I do not consider that my assessment of the evidence of either of the parties was compromised by the different modes utilised for the giving of that evidence or the different settings from which it was given.
As a number of judges of this Court have observed,[4] the Court is well able to assess the evidence of witnesses given electronically, and such a method of giving evidence does not impact the capacity of the Court to assess the demeanour and veracity of a witness or to allow cross-examination to proceed effectively.
The ICL submitted that there had been no qualitative difference in the ability of the Court to assess the parties as witnesses, as the Court had had a clear view of the mother and her facial expressions and there had been no delay in transmission, meaning that the immediacy of her reactions had been clear. I agree.
Ms C gave evidence and was cross-examined by counsel for each of the mother and the ICL. She gave her evidence in a forthright and fulsome manner and freely volunteered extensive information, including information which was clearly difficult for her to share, often in a manner which far exceeded the scope of the question she had been asked. Ms C impressed an earnest witness endeavouring to provide honest answers. However, the weight that can be given to her evidence is limited by her unwavering support of and strongly protective approach towards the father, and her lack of understanding of or indeed interest in the experiences reported by the mother and many of the risk issues raised in the proceedings.
In accordance with section 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth), all findings of fact in this judgment are made on the balance of probabilities. Some of the findings in these reasons relate to conduct of a very serious nature, including findings of serious acts of family violence. I have had regard to the matters outlined in subsection 140(2) of the Evidence Act, particularly the gravity of the matters alleged, when making such findings, and am satisfied to the extent of “actual persuasion”[5] with respect to each of my findings with respect to such matters.

THE RELEVANT LEGAL PRINCIPLES

The legislation

Part VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) guides the process of decision-making in relation to the making of parenting orders. Section 60CA of the Act provides that when deciding whether to make a particular parenting order in relation to a child, the Court must consider the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration. Section 60CC of the Act sets out how those best interests are to be determined, and includes a list of considerations to be taken into account.

What arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of the child and each person who has care of the child

As the Full Court of this Court (“the Full Court”) held in Pierce & Pierce (No 2),[6] “the construction of Pt VII of the Act ... places a consideration of the safety of a child or another person as a focused starting point in the considerations being weighed in determining a parenting regime that best promotes the best interests of the child...”
Furthermore, as observed by the Full Court in Fiedler & Vitale:[7]
[w]hilst ...no one consideration prescribed by s 60CC(2) is determinative of a child’s best interests, it is difficult to fathom a circumstance in which s 60CC(2)(a) would not be given prominence in the line of reasoning, if the Court was of the view that a child was not safe and/or where the child was at risk of some form of harm....

Meaning of safety

The word “safety” is not defined in the Act. It is defined in the Oxford English Dictionary as “the state of being protected from or guarded against hurt or injury; freedom from danger.”
In Oyama & Oyama,[8] Gill J held that “safety” in this context is a term that is wide in nature, and bounded only by its object, being to keep children, and those who care for them, from harm, and that it should not be taken to have a fixed content or standard. His Honour held that the paragraph requires the Court to consider the risk of harm that accompanies the relationship, in whatever form it may take and does not contemplate consideration of the benefits of a relationship where such is not safe.
In Denton & Denton (No 3),[9] Altobelli J expressed the view that “safety” is a state in which hazards or conditions leading to physical, psychological, or material harm are controlled in order to preserve the health and wellbeing of an individual. It is not the complete elimination of risk of harm, but rather making such order as affords the child the most optimal protection from harm.
In Melounis & Melounis (No 4),[10] Altobelli J held that to promote safety, for the purposes of subsection 60CC(2)(a), means “to advance the interests of, and move to a stronger or more prominent position, the safety (ie to be free from hurt, damage, harm or danger) of children and those who care for them” and that the statutory requirement to promote safety involves advancing a positive outcome, which would necessarily include protection from harm but would not be limited to this.
I respectfully adopt these definitions in determining the arrangements that would promote X’s safety and that of her primary caregiver, being the mother.

Family violence

In considering this factor, it is necessary for the Court to consider any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child) and any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.[11]
“Family violence” is defined in section 4AB of the Act to mean “violent, threatening or other behaviour by a person that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family (the family member), or causes the family member to be fearful.” It is not necessary that violent or threatening behaviour coerce or control or cause fear in order to be characterised as family violence.[12]
The mother’s allegations of conduct on the part of the father constituting family violence included the following:
(a) Threatening to rape her.

(b) Attending at her home without her consent following X’s birth, becoming verbally abusive, and threatening her and the maternal grandmother, causing them to contact both the police and the mother’s stepfather out of fear for their safety.

(c) Sending her numerous text messages threatening to harm and kill her.

(d) Deliberately using her past traumatic experiences to taunt her, and to provoke a trauma response and incite fear in order to control her. As an example of this, the mother alleged that on one occasion after the parties engaged in sexual intercourse, the father told her that he was “just like her father,” knowing that she had been subjected to sexual abuse by her father.

(e) Using references to his past membership of a gang and his related associates to intimidate her.

(f) Calling her abusive and derogatory names such as “slut,” “cunt” and “piece of shit.”

(g) Threatening to hurt her if she reported his abuse to police.

(h) Arranging for friends or associates to stalk and harass her.

(i) Sitting in his car outside her home and watching her, and having his friends do so, and communicating with her in a manner designed to ensure that she knew she was being watched, such as by commenting on the frequency with which she had been out the front to smoke cigarettes.

(j) Sending her a text message in the lead up to X’s birth saying:

put my girl in danger. U won't hear from me just waiting 4 ur call to say ur ready until then I'm over ur moods and shit! See u on the way to hospital until then safe & happy I'm out ur life. Just remember RESPECT ALL FEAR NOBODY! LIKE ME OR HATE ME EITHER WAY GET USE TO ME. in two days from 2day tomorrow will be yesterday .... (As per the original)
(k) Sending her a message on 1 November 2020 making reference to two former partners of the mother, including B’s father, MR K, who had shot the mother in the kneecap, saying:
Prepare a visit 2nite when ur in the boot think how [Mr L] fucked sluts & so did [Mr K] & shot u in the leg. I never fucked sluts & I'll miss ur leg & like iv said get u in the head! U caused this I'm furious drunk at the pokies planing it with my fellas sou have the money and ring [...] & tell her to expect me at 11 so ring the cops dog they better gard u cause the minute there gone so are u!! U better not lie about the money or someone was in ur house or I promise I'll make it a slow D_ h! Time to tell the truth??[13]

(As per the original)
(l) Sending her a text message in November 2020 (the month of X’s birth) saying

“Good skank don't worry about what ur after child surport worry about life surport” (As per the original).

(m) Making a Facebook post in November 2021,[14] which contained a photograph of the mother and said:

If anybody knows the cunt [Ms Shaul] please let me know cause the piece of Shit won’t let me see my Daughter it’s been 1 year now that I’m not allowed to see her, my fucken blood just let me know??

(As per the original)
(n) Leaving a handwritten note on her front doorstep threatening to kill her.

(o) Threatening to kidnap B and murder her and telling the mother she could watch her bleed out.

(p) Subjecting her to erratic behaviour including outbursts of anger.

Police records also record that the mother received a message on 11 July 2021, from a number that was not known to her but which belonged to a person who informed police that the father had used his phone, saying:
Bitch stop Stopping [Mr Dayan] to see his daughter or cunt Ill take urs to see want it feels like so u cant see urs & feel his bleeding heart then Ill come take u away from BOTH ur kids & kill u so [MR DAYAN] is at peace without u stopping a good man see & feel his girls! Last warning Bitch Cunt do the right thing before I get u. That’s a Promise!!! Count ur Days Skank bye bye

(As per the original)
With respect to the text messages referred to above, the father acknowledged that the phone number that appeared on the printed versions of the messages was his, but denied having sent them and demanded “proof,” insinuating that the mother had fabricated them. Police records record that police officers viewed at least two of the threatening messages directly on the mother’s phone.[15]
The father asserted that he could not have written the text messages in question because he could not write. Ms C gave evidence to similar effect, but also confirmed that she and the father had commenced their relationship by exchanging written messages. The assessment of Dr P, who assessed the father’s cognitive functioning as part of his psychological assessment, was that the father would likely have the capacity to write to the standard required to create the messages in question. I am not satisfied that the father’s literacy level is such that it would not be possible for him to have authored the messages.
In his trial affidavit, father admitted to having made the Facebook post referred to at subparagraph 44(m) above. Under cross-examination, he repeated that admission but then sought to retreat from it by claiming that somebody else (being a person he was unable to name) had typed and posted it at his direction. The difference is immaterial.
It was put to the mother that if she had in fact received the handwritten letter referred to at subparagraph 44(n) above, she would have kept it. She responded that she had given it to the police. It was then put to her that she had not in fact given it to the police, a proposition directly contradicted by the [16]levant police records,16 and then that she should have taken a photograph of it. She credibly explained that her concern at the time had been with the threat to her life and the safety of herself and her children, rather than evidence gathering.
The mother alleged that the father’s abuse and threats towards her had at times occurred in the presence of B, who was six years old at the conclusion of the parties’ relationship.
The mother’s evidence of family violence included the use of coercive and controlling behaviours by the father. The concept of coercive and controlling violence has been described as “a course of conduct aimed at dominating and controlling another person,”[17] and as “the exercise of power, to restrain another or to cause another to act, by force, domination or command.”[18] It is a particularly insidious form of family violence.
The concept of coercive control essentially captures one person’s domination over another. A person may subject another to coercive control without subjectively intending to do so. Intention is not a necessary element of coercive or controlling behaviour. A person who engages in such behaviour may be completely oblivious to the impact of their behaviour, but the behaviour may nevertheless coerce or control the other family member and fall within the definition of family violence. The focus is on the behaviour and its impact. A finding that there is coercive or controlling behaviour is an evaluative one, having regard to all of the circumstances and all of the evidence before the court.[19]
As Aldridge and Carew JJ held in Pickford & Pickford:[20]
The focus of the fact finding process is on the behaviour and the impact of the behaviour. It is the behaviour that coerces or controls. It requires action and reaction. A single act is unlikely to be coercive or controlling but it may be. Behaviour that coerces or controls may be innocuous, subtle, capable of different interpretations, complex, undermining, etc. Such behaviour may create impossible expectations for the other family member. It may be transactional and involve punishments for perceived failures. The impact of the behaviour can be insidious. A confident and happy family member may lose their confidence, their self-esteem, question their sanity, shun their family and friends, become depressed, irritable, inefficient, and unhappy. There may be times when they strike out either verbally or physically.
Consistently with these descriptions, Ms J opined that the father’s conduct as described by the mother:
... appears consistent with established patterns of family violence and coercive control, wherein the perpetrator engages in a range of abusive behaviours such as verbal abuse, intimidation, threats, and harassment with the intention of instilling compliance with their wishes and views, thereby exerting control over the victim.
Ms J also recorded that:
The risk of serious harm for victims and children can significantly increase once the victim/survivor has left the relationship as the perpetrator resorts to more extreme and, hence, dangerous behaviours to exert power and control. This appeared consistent with [Ms Shaul’s] allegations regarding the escalation of [Mr Dayan’s] harassment, intimidation and threats once she ceased contact with him
And further that “[t]he alleged pattern of coercive and threatening conduct suggests a sustained effort to exert power and control, with the potential for psychological and physical harm.”
Section 60CG of the Act provides that in considering what order to make, the Court must, to the extent that it is possible to do so consistently with the child’s best interests being the paramount consideration, ensure that the order is consistent with any family violence order and does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence.
In December 2020, the mother sought and obtained an interim family violence order against the father. A final order was made in June 2021 on an uncontested basis. The order names the father as the defendant and the mother as the protected person. X is not named on the order despite having been included in the mother’s application for the order.[21] The father’s evidence was that he had “successfully applied” to have X’s name removed from the order. He produced no documents to support that assertion. The mother deposed to having agreed to having the order made with respect to her only in order to avoid the matter proceeding to a contested hearing, on the basis that X (and B) would be protected by virtue of being in her care. The mother credibly denied that she had agreed to the removal of X’s name because she had known that the father was not a risk to X. I accept the mother’s evidence in this regard.
In November 2021, the father was charged with breaching the family violence order. His evidence was that this was a result of a social media post which “the mother had wrongfully perceived as a threat.” He deposed that he attended Court and “explained” this to the magistrate, and the charge was dismissed. The mother’s evidence was that the post in question had called her a “cunt” and said he “wanted [her] head” but that the police had asserted that it would be difficult to secure a conviction.
In January 2021, the mother moved with B and X into emergency accommodation provided by a domestic violence service.
The mother’s evidence was to the effect that she permitted the father to spend time with X in April 2021 under duress, out of fear of the consequences of refusing to do so arising from intimidation, threats and harassment from him. She deposed that safety concerns arising from his erratic behaviour led her to cease facilitating the time after approximately one month. She also deposed that the father had become aware of her new address through a friend of hers and at that time had recommenced an earlier practice of having associates drive past and watch her home.
The father’s evidence was that the mother had telephoned him “out of the blue” at 2.00 am one morning and offered him time with X. He deposed that he then saw X each day for approximately one week, after which the mother suddenly stopped responding to his communications and facilitating time with X, and he had “no idea why.”
Because of my comparative assessment of the evidence of the parties, and because of its consistency with documentary evidence of contemporaneous events including the mother’s application for a family violence order and move into emergency housing, I accept the mother’s evidence with respect to the circumstances surrounding the father spending time with X in April 2021.
The mother alleged that once she ceased facilitating the time, the father’s threatening and abusive behaviour escalated, leading her to obtain further emergency housing in April 2021.
The father denied the mother’s allegations of family violence, asserting that they were baseless and had been fabricated. This denial included a denial of having sent the text messages referred to above. In the witness box, he acknowledged that violence would include verbal violence and harassment, and asserted that he had “never had violence with anybody in [his] life.”
The father denied in his affidavit that he had ever laughed in response to incidents of violence. Whilst under cross-examination, he laughed in response the incident of violence represented by the text message referred to at subparagraph 44(k) hereof.
The father suggested to Ms J that the mother had fabricated the allegations she made against him as an act of retribution after he had rebuffed an attempt made by her to reconcile in April 2021. This theory is difficult to reconcile with the fact that the mother’s first move into emergency housing and her application for the family violence order both took place prior to April 2021.
In support of his denial of having sent the message referred to at subparagraph 44(k) above, the father claimed that he had not been aware that “[Mr K]” was the name of the mother’s former partner until that information was put to him in cross-examination. The name of the mother’s former partner had appeared in numerous documents produced in these proceedings prior to the commencement of the trial, including Dr P’s report with respect to the mother, Ms J’s family report, the mother’s trial affidavit and the ICL’s case outline. It had also appeared in the affidavit made by the mother in support of her application for [22]family violence order.22 Most significantly, it appeared in the father’s own trial affidavit. The father’s evidence in this regard was extremely unconvincing, even allowing for his cognitive limitations.
The mother’s evidence with respect to the father’s propensity for angry outbursts was supported by:
(a) The father’s own report to Dr O that he is occasionally angry and irritable. Under cross-examination, he asserted that he could not remember how his anger and irritability manifested.

(b) The father’s own report to Dr P that, in relation to anger, “I let it out and sometimes I explode.” Under cross-examination, he was unable or unwilling to explain what he had meant by that.

(c) Dr H’s report that the father has been prone to outbursts of rage and anger when frustrated by situations contrary to his wishes or when his desires are thwarted.

(d) The father’s presentation at times in while giving his evidence, during which he appeared to be struggling to contain his anger on a number of occasions. He presented at times as heightened, agitated, confrontational, defensive and aggressive, and at times raised his voice. This presentation contradicted his assertion to Dr P that he had “never even yelled at another person.”

Similarly consistent with the mother’s allegations were the following elements of the father’s profile according to the Personality Assessment Inventory (“PAI”) administered by Dr P:
(a) His profile was significantly elevated for “attitude to aggression” and “physical aggression.” Dr P reported that such a profile indicated that the father was a person who experienced much ongoing anger, and who, in situations of conflict, was likely to express his anger directly in a physical rather than verbal manner. When cross-examined about this, the father said it “depends on the situation” and if somebody attacked his loved ones he would “react in any way that was suited for the situation.”

(b) On the interpersonal scales, the father was very high on both the “dominance” and “warmth” scales. Dr P explained that the father’s interpersonal style was best characterised as involving a very strong need for attention and affiliation, which may result in him being perceived by others as controlling, and may take the form of him interfering with social interactions in order to meet his personal needs. Dr P opined that the father’s need for attention may be so strong that any opportunity to interact with others will be acted upon, as long as the interaction allows the father to have some control over the relationship.

(c) On the violence potential index scale, the father’s level of acute risk was assessed as “high risk” compared to the “community” sample and “moderate-high risk” compared to the “clinical norm sample.”

The father was cross-examined about police records demonstrating that police had responded to allegations made by two former partners against him with respect to repeated unwanted attendances at their homes and refusal to leave when asked, harassment, and aggressive and intimidating behaviour similar to that alleged by the mother.[23] He asserted that he was unable to remember most of the incidents in question. If that evidence was true, it suggests that the father’s denials of having perpetrated family violence can, at best, be considered evidence only that he does not remember having done so. Concerningly, he asked what these incidents had to do with domestic violence. His denials of having engaged in family violence must be considered in the context of this lack of understanding that such behaviour constitutes family violence.
It was put to the mother that she had not taken any threats she had received from the father seriously. She gave compelling evidence that this was not the case and that she had taken them very seriously. As the mother herself pointed out, her actions in making reports to police, applying for a family violence order, engaging with social workers and moving with her young children to emergency housing on two occasions support her evidence in this regard.
It was then put to the mother that she had not taken the threats seriously throughout 2020 because she had not done anything about them at that time, and when she had eventually done something, all she had initially done was block the father’s phone number. The mother gave tearful, highly emotional and compelling evidence of having been in a state of fear, having been frightened to do anything, and not having known what the “right” reaction was. This evidence encapsulated the reality is that there is no “right” reaction to family violence.
The premise of this line of questioning was out of step with contemporary understandings of family violence. Long gone are (or at least should be) the days when a person reporting an experience of family violence is asked a question to the effect of “if it was so bad, why didn’t you just leave?”.
As the Full Court made clear in Amador & Amador,[24] victims of family violence do not have to complain to authorities in order to have their accounts accepted. Nor do they have to take other identifiable steps in response to their experiences. I reject the suggestion that anything about the mother’s actions during 2020 or at any other time constitutes evidence that she did not take the father’s threatening behaviour seriously.
Furthermore, I accept and endorse the submission made on behalf of the ICL that to suggest that the mother did not take the threats seriously is to misunderstand and minimise the objectively serious nature of the threats themselves and the insidious and manipulative manner in which the mother’s own trauma history was woven into those threats to cause her maximum harm.
Ms C gave evidence that she had never witnessed the father commit an act of family violence including physical aggression, verbal abuse or coercive control. I note that Ms C is in an intact and apparently stable relationship with the father. Her experience does not indicate that the mother’s evidence is false, and nor does it provide a reliable indicator of the manner in which the father would be likely to behave towards the mother, a person towards he continues, even during his evidence, to exhibit considerable animus, in the event of contact with her in future.
I accept the mother’s allegations of family violence in their entirety and find that the father subjected her to violent, abusive, intimidating and threatening behaviour and to coercive control.
The mother gave clear, unequivocal and compelling evidence, which I accept, that she experienced and continues to experience intense fear of the father. She became tearful as she described him as scary, unpredictable and dangerous and described feeling overwhelmed with fear and anxiety at the prospect of being required to hand X over to him and her belief that it would only be “a matter of time” before something bad happened to X.
She also gave evidence that following the service of the father’s court documents, she had become afraid that the father would find her again and that B, who had overheard the process server mentioning the father’s name, had been afraid to play outside the house, fearing that the father had located them.
The mother gave evidence of deliberate steps she has taken to ensure that the father cannot locate her and her children, including distancing herself from people who know him and requesting that his former solicitor not disclose her residential address (at which she was served after being located by a private investigator) to him. She described having worked hard to create a safe, nurturing and secure environment for X to grow and develop in, free from exposure to unsafe persons from her past, and to being concerned about the risk of undermining this through X’s exposure to the father, whom she considers unsafe.
I accept the mother’s evidence that the father’s actions caused and continue to cause her fear.
Counsel for the father repeatedly put to the mother that her view of the father had been tainted by her past trauma, that it was her past experiences and not anything done by the father that caused her to be fearful, and that she was confusing or conflating the father with other abusive men she had encountered in her life. She firmly and credibly denied this and explained that the father had used her past trauma to instil fear and control her, but that it was his actions, and not her past experiences, which caused her to fear him.
Counsel for the father repeatedly, in his cross-examination and in his closing submissions, emphasised that the mother’s evidence did not disclose “actual physical violence.” To the extent that this can be taken to imply that violence must be physical in order to constitute “actual” violence, this suggestion must be rejected in the strongest of terms.
As submitted by counsel for the ICL, the definition of family violence in the Act encompasses far more than simply physical violence, and many of the acts in which I have found the father engaged constitute serious and concerning family violence. It must be recognised that threatening, intimidating and abusive behaviour and coercive control are insidious behaviours that can cause serious and lasting psychological and emotional consequences whether they are accompanied by acts of physical violence or not.
It is true that the mother’s affidavit did not contain evidence of physical violence perpetrated against her by the father. The only evidence before the Court of an allegation of physical violence made by the mother against the father is a statement made by the mother to Ms J to the effect that the father had used excessive physical force towards her in the form of using his body weight to pin her down. This allegation did not appear in the mother’s trial affidavit and was not put to the father in cross-examination. I accept the submission of counsel for the father that in those circumstances, the evidence before the Court does not support a finding that this incident occurred. However, this is not the same as being able to find that it did not occur, and I do not find that the mother was untruthful in the account she gave to Ms J.
It is a well-recognised feature of the experience of trauma and family violence that victims often do not provide a comprehensive, consistent and cohesive account of their experiences on each occasion upon which they tell their story. This issue was addressed by the Full Court in Hendy & Penningh,[25] where their Honours said the following:
...it seems that the approach to the evidence given by the mother, including under cross-examination, was that unless, when she (or Z) first spoke about family violence they gave a comprehensive account (including dates and times) of all that occurred, anything disclosed later was viewed with suspicion. Indeed anything said after the initial disclosure that was not included in the initial disclosure was treated as inconsistent with that disclosure. Inconsistency having thus been established, the totality of the evidence as to family violence was then evaluated as unreliable. No attempt was made to identify a core consistency in what she said. Amongst other things, this approach fails to recognise how hard it is for victims of violence inflicted by a family member, who is often someone they love or on whom they rely for sustenance, to speak about what has happened. The subject matter is distressing and giving voice to what has occurred can be traumatic in itself. We cannot say strongly enough that those involved in cases such as this must bear in mind that in this setting, disclosure is a process that often requires time before a complete picture emerges.
The father’s police records demonstrate that at least one former partner other than the mother has made an allegation of physical violence against him in the form of an allegation that he had smashed in her door by kicking it and physically assaulted her by punching her in the face.[26] The Court is similarly not in a position to make any finding with respect to that allegation, and I merely observe that it is simply not the case that the father has never been accused of having committed physical family violence.
Most significantly, it must be said that the absence of a finding of physical violence (or, for that matter, a finding that physical violence did not occur) categorically does not equate to a finding that a person has not engaged in any family violence, that they have not engaged in serious and dangerous family violence, or that they do not pose a risk to a child. It is also the case that the absence of a finding that physical violence has occurred in the past does not equate to a finding that there is no risk of physical violence occurring in future, particularly in circumstances such as those present here, where there have been overt and explicit threats of physical violence.
Counsel for the father submitted that even if the threats alleged by the mother had been made (a finding he conceded was open), they had never been acted upon, because the father had not committed physical violence. This submission misses the point that it is the threats themselves and the fear they instil that constitutes the family violence. Failure to act upon threats does not render those threats harmless, meaningless or inconsequential. Acting upon them would constitute further family violence in addition to the violence already committed by having made them. The mother gave evidence, and I accept, that the father’s threats had left her certain that it was “only a matter of time.” That in itself is a serious harm inflicted by the father upon her. The fact that the father did not commit even more family violence does not detract from the seriousness of the family violence he did commit. Evidence that a person has made but not acted upon threats of serious violence is hardly evidence that that person is safe.
Ms J confirmed in the witness box that the absence of evidence of physical violence did not reduce her concerns about family violence in any significant way.
Many of the instances of family violence to which the father subjected the mother demonstrate misogynistic views and concerning and demeaning attitudes towards women. This, particularly when considered in light of the limitations to the father’s impulse control, raises concerns about the kind of role model the father would be for X and the views and attitudes to which she would be exposed if she were to spend time or communicate with him. There is a risk that time and communication with the father could result in X being exposed to his harmful attitudes towards women.
In her family report, Ms J expressed concern about “the potential dangerousness and lethality” of the father’s abuse. She opined that the cumulative nature of the allegations give rise to significant concerns regarding his capacity for coercive control and the potential risk he may pose to the safety and well-being of X, the mother, and B. Similarly, Dr P assessed the father’s potential violence as “an ongoing and significant concern.”
The father’s ongoing denial of having engaged in family violence, even in the face of clear evidence, and even in the face of his own admissions in respect of the Facebook post made in November 2021, which was an act of family violence, is itself a matter of concern. Denial and minimisation of events found to have occurred is relevant to assessment of risk.[27] Ms J opined, and I accept, that:
In the event of substantiation of the allegations of abuse and coercive control, [Mr Dayan’s] ongoing denial represents a significant risk factor, as it suggests a failure to acknowledge the severity of his actions and their impact. The lack of accountability for one's behaviours indicates a poor prognosis for positive behaviour change and insufficient insight into the impact of one’s abusive or harmful behaviours on others, both of which suggest the potential for harmful and abusive behaviours to be repeated in the future. As such, this raises significant concern about the ongoing risk of harm to [X], [B] [sic] and [Ms Shaul].
To Dr P, the father indicated that he did not know why the parties’ relationship had ended, and that the mother had simply “ghosted” him. He gave evidence to similar effect in the witness box. The father also told Dr P that his violent history had been 20 years ago, that he characterised himself as “a loving person who was no longer violent” and that he did not know why the mother did not want him to see X.
If that is indeed an accurate description of the father’s understanding of the circumstances of the parties’ separation and the level of his violence towards the mother, it is indicative of a significant lack of insight and a concerning lack of accountability for his actions. It also leaves the Court with no basis whatsoever for any confidence that there will be any meaningful change to the father’s behaviour should he be permitted to interact with the mother in future.
In his trial affidavit, the father claimed to “deeply regret” having called the mother a “cunt” and a “piece of shit,” yet that expression of remorse was followed immediately by attempts to justify his behaviour and attribute blame for it to the mother by saying that it had occurred because was “very upset and angry that the mother was unnecessarily depriving [him] of a relationship with [X].” Despite his asserted deep regret, the father once again called the mother a “piece of shit” during his oral evidence and said he would “stand by that 100 per cent.” Under cross-examination, he denied having called her a “cunt” (though he also admitted having done so). I do not accept that the father feels any remorse with respect to his violent behaviour or that he has any real understanding of its harmful impact.
Further evidence of the father’s lack of understanding of, or willingness to take accountability for, his harmful actions was provided in the form of his response when the contents of the text message referred to at subparagraph 44(k) above were put to him. When the deeply concerning words of that message were read to him, he facetiously responded “is that poetry?” and then laughed. The mother gave compelling evidence that this response from the father had caused her distress as it had triggered memories of lack of the empathy she had shown towards her.
Ms J opined that the father’s response in this regard was minimising and showed a lack of regard for the seriousness of the conduct, which heightened the risk for X. Even if the father genuinely does not recall having sent that message and believes he did not do so, his glib response to its disturbing contents is a matter of serious concern.
The father’s flippancy with respect to this issue was described as being “at the heart of” the ICL’s concerns with respect to the father’s attitude towards family violence: he simply does not take it seriously. I am satisfied that the father either does not understand or does not care about the harm he has inflicted on the mother.
Dr P gave evidence that he held concerns about the father’s insight into his own behaviour, and about his insight into how he was viewed by others. No part of the father’s evidence provided any basis for the alleviation of such concerns. The consequence of this is that there is no basis for expecting any meaningful change in future.

Father’s criminal history and criminal associations

The father has an extensive criminal history involving offences including driving whilst disqualified, driving under the influence of methamphetamine, other drug-related offences, failure to comply with bonds and bail conditions, and carrying an offensive weapon. In 2010 and 2017 he served sentences of imprisonment.[28]
The father is also currently facing further charges relating to possession of a prohibited weapon, being a knife, arising from a traffic stop in late 2025.[29] He did not disclose this fact to the Court and it was only discovered upon the making of an order pursuant to section 67ZBD of the Act directed to South Australia Police at the commencement of the trial.
Under protection of a certificate granted pursuant to section 128 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth), the father admitted that he had had a knife in his bag when stopped by police. In an attempt to explain an entry in the relevant police record indicating that he had been argumentative with police, he alleged that the police had stolen money from him and asserted that he had had $20,000 in cash on him at the time. He provided no convincing evidence as to how he, as a pensioner, had been in possession of such a sum of money, but said that “maybe” he had been going to buy a car. This evidence was entirely unconvincing.
The father asserted implausibly under cross-examination that his police records were not correct and did not accurately reflect his criminal history, suggesting that somebody with the same name and/or his cousin was responsible for some of the criminal offending attributed to him. He also professed to be unable to understand what his criminal history had to do with his application for time with X, which demonstrated a concerning lack of insight.
Ms J opined, and I accept, that the father’s criminal history gives rise to concerns about his potential for unpredictable and harmful behaviour, poor judgment on his behalf and his willingness to act outside established legal and social norms in order to fulfil his demands and desires. I also accept Ms J’s evidence that the pattern of behaviours represented by the father’s criminal history raises questions about his ability to prioritise parenting responsibilities and maintain a lifestyle that promotes the safety and well-being of a child.
The identified risk is compounded by the father’s lack of demonstrated understanding or acknowledgement of the seriousness of his criminal behaviour, which leaves little room for confidence of meaningful change in the future. The father’s lack of acknowledgment may, to some extent, be affected by his difficulties with memory. He acknowledged to Dr P that he had spent time in custody but said he could not recall any details about his past offending. Nonetheless, in the absence of meaningful acceptance and commitment for change, this remains a serious risk factor.
It is an undisputed fact that the father was previously a member of a gang. He asserted that he had left that gang in 2006 or 2007 following the birth of his son, out of a desire to distance himself from the associated dangerous lifestyle. The evidence does not suggest otherwise and I accept the father’s evidence in this regard.
However, the mother alleged that the father continued to associate with members or former members of the gang and that he had used these associations to threaten and intimidate her. As a result of my assessment of the mother’s evidence more broadly, and because it is consistent with the preponderance of the evidence before the Court with respect to the father’s conduct, I accept the mother’s evidence in this regard.

Father’s mental health and functioning

The father has been diagnosed with Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (“ADHD”), Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (“PTSD”), and multiple mental health disorders. The evidence before the Court indicates that he also suffers anxiety (including panic attacks) and depression.
The father is prescribed and takes a number of medications relating to his mental health conditions, including an antipsychotic.
Dr O reported the following:
(a) The father’s facial expressions and body language did not indicate that he was suffering from a severe psychiatric disorder.

(b) There were no other observable signs (such as self-neglect) indicative of him suffering from a severe psychiatric disorder.

(c) The range and amplitude of the father's affect were within normal limits. His affect was appropriately responsive and reactive. He was able to appropriately interact emotionally and to establish rapport.

(d) The father’s memory and concentration were impaired and his intellectual functioning compromised.

(e) The father’s judgment seemed compromised.

(f) There were no psychotic symptoms such as delusions or perceptual abnormalities.

To Dr O, the father reported experiencing flashbacks of being injured.
Dr O considered, based on a reasonable degree of medical probability, that the father was suffering PTSD and ADHD. He was unable to confirm the father’s existing diagnosis of a mental health disorder because the father was unable to remember the symptoms he had experienced. Dr O also considered it likely that the father was suffering from a brain disorder.
Dr H reported that the father’s symptoms include chronic auditory hallucinations and some paranoid ideas at times. He reported that he can also be prone to suicidal thoughts, mood difficulties, anger, anxiety and panic attacks. Dr H reported that the father’s condition was relatively stable on regular medication and that in recent years (clarified by Dr H in the witness box to mean the past four to five years), the most prominent problems had been anxiety and panic attacks. Although the father’s auditory hallucinations were chronic, he had not complained about them to Dr H for some time.
A complicating factor when considering the impact of the father’s mental health on the issues requiring determination is the unreliability of the father’s account of his experience of psychiatric symptoms. For example, he informed Dr O that he had not experienced psychiatric symptoms beyond flashbacks, anxiety and depression in recent times, but in the witness box, he gave evidence of experiencing auditory hallucinations “pretty much” every day, including during the course of giving his evidence.
The father also expressed a view to Dr O that there is “nothing” wrong with him. Dr P gave evidence that this view held by the father would exacerbate the risk he posed.
The impact of the father’s brain injury and mental health on X’s safety and that of the mother is an issue squarely raised on the evidence before the Court. The father expressed concern that his mental illness might constitute a barrier to the development of a relationship between himself and X, and was at pains to emphasise that this was an issue that was not his fault and was beyond his control. The father exhibited very significant obviously genuine distress in relation to this issue when he gave his evidence.
I accept that the father is not responsible for the fact that he suffers mental illness. I also accept that he has acted appropriately and responsibly in relation to his mental health, including by way of reliable attendance upon Dr H and undertaking the treatment recommended and the medication prescribed by Dr H and other medical professionals over a very long period of time. To Ms J, the father described deliberate engagement in self-regulation strategies to manage the symptoms of his PTSD.
The line between mental health conditions and brain injuries on the one hand, and the behaviour of the sufferer on the other, is not always clearly drawn. It is not necessary or appropriate to speculate about the extent to which the father might, in the past, or in the future, behave or have behaved differently, had he not experienced the brain injury or his mental health conditions, save to note that all or almost all of the father’s association with the outlaw gang (at least as reported by him) and a substantial number of the offences which form part of his criminal history occurred prior to both his injury and the reported onset of at least the most serious of his mental health conditions.
As the Full Court held in Oberlin & Infeld,[30] any diagnosis with a particular psychological condition does not of itself necessarily mean the party lacks sufficient parenting capacity, rather, it is necessary to determine if and how the condition and behaviour influences the necessary findings under section 60CC of the Act with respect to the child’s best interests.
In the present case, what is important from the perspective of the Court’s determination of X’s best interests is not the extent of the father’s mental ill health and injury but his behaviour and the risk he poses as a consequence of that behaviour to X and the mother. Violent and threatening conduct constitutes a risk and gives rise to the need for caution whether the person engaging in that conduct suffers from mental illness or functioning deficits or not.
Were it the case that the father suffered from his various mental health conditions and brain injury but had not engaged in violent, threatening or otherwise dangerous behaviour, it is quite possible that the primary focus for the Court would be how, rather than whether, X and the father could safely spend time together and enjoy a relationship. However, that is not the factual matrix with which the Court is faced.
I repeat and refer to my earlier findings with respect to the father’s violent and threatening behaviour. It is this behaviour, and the risk of such behaviour occurring again in future, and not the father’s diagnoses per se, that pose the greatest risk to X’s safety and that of the mother.
There are other aspects of the father’s conduct which may or may not be related to his mental health, which, in conjunction with his violent and abusive conduct, increase the risk he poses to the safety of X and the mother.
One example of this is a tendency to become fixated and obsessive. Ms J observed that when observed by her, the father presented as fixated on his connection to X.
The mother alleged that the father had become fixated on B despite only having known her for a very brief period of time. She alleged that this had manifested in conduct such as displaying a photograph of her in his home (which the father accepted was accurate), repeatedly asking the mother to leave her in his care, and commenting “my two sexy girls” on a social media post depicting the mother and B. Consistently with the mother’s allegation, the father claimed in his trial affidavit that he “loved” B despite the very limited duration of his association with her.
The father also presented with elements of fixation in the witness box. One example of this was his repeated reference to a suggestion in Ms J’s report to Ms C’s children having been removed from her care as a result of sexual abuse, when in fact the removal had occurred as a result of physical abuse. The father persistently and repeatedly complained about this issue, even when it had no or only peripheral relevance to the question he had been asked.
Similarly, the father returned repeatedly to a reference in police records about which he was cross-examined to reference an address in Suburb M. The address in question was of no relevance to the proceeding but the father was unable to disregard it and move on with his answers to questions.
Another aspect of the father’s presentation suggested by the evidence before the Court is a tendency towards impulsive behaviour. The mother gave evidence that the father can “just snap like that.” Dr P reported (based on PAI analysis) that impulsivity and recklessness were features of the father’s personality profile.
Dr H reported that the father experienced poor impulse control, intense outbursts of rage and anger, low frustration tolerance and violent acting out, though Dr H reported improvement with self-control and emotional regulation following the commencement of stimulant medication in December 2022. Dr H clarified under cross-examination that the intense outbursts of rage and anger referred to in his report had been verbal in nature.
A related concern raised with respect to the father was a poor understanding of, and/or lack of respect for, appropriate boundaries. In this regard, Ms J noted that the alleged (and now established) sustained harassment of the mother through repeated, unsolicited, and abusive communications, stalking behaviours and having enlisted his associates to perpetrate similar abusive, threatening and intimidating behaviours towards the mother suggested an inability on the father’s part to disengage appropriately and a persistent intrusion into the mother’s boundaries.
This is consistent with the father’s alleged behaviour towards other former partners as described in the police records.[31]
In his affidavit evidence and in the witness box, the father demonstrated no insight into this issue, which leaves the Court with no basis for any expectation of improvement in the future.
The likely impact of the father’s personality profile and other vulnerabilities (particularly when considered in light of his violent history and difficulties with impulse control) in the context of the challenges associated with building a relationship with X from a starting point of no pre-existing relationship, in circumstances in which the father is for all intents and purposes a stranger to X, occupied significant time during the course of the trial.
As submitted on behalf of the ICL, it is not known how X would react to meeting and spending time with the father. To his credit, the father acknowledged that meeting him could be confusing, stressful and even scary for X. He expressed a hope that X would gain an understanding from spending time with him that he was “not a monster.” He also acknowledged that knowing that her mother was afraid of the father and having to keep secrets, such as her address, from him while trying to build a relationship, would be a burden for X.
The particular risk emphasised by the mother and the ICL in this regard was the risk of how the father might react if X did not embrace a relationship with him in the manner he hoped, or if her reactions to meeting and spending time with him did not meet his likely idealised expectations.
Dr P, who administered the PAI to the father, reported that the father had significant problems in thinking and concentration, accompanied by prominent hostility, resentment and suspiciousness; that sensitivity in social interactions had likely served as a formidable obstacle to his development of close relationships, and he was likely to be cautious, withdrawn and isolated, feeling both estranged from and mistreated by people around him. In the witness box, he said that the father “ticks all the red flag boxes about risk” including potential for violence and potential for emotional instability. Dr H agreed with respect to resentment and suspiciousness, though he was clear that these features were not always present in the father.
Dr P further reported that the father’s judgment was very poor (poor judgment being a feature also reported as likely by Dr O and referred to by Dr H) and that his interpersonal relationships were likely to be superficial, volatile and short-lived. This latter observation is certainly true of the father’s relationship with the mother, though I note that he is currently in an enduring and apparently stable relationship with Ms C.
In the witness box, Dr P opined that if X were to “act up,” it was likely that the father would react negatively, that he had limited flexibility, and that this could place X at risk, including psychological and emotional risk. Dr P agreed that the making of orders for time would constitute placing X in an experiment.
Dr H gave evidence that there could be a negative outcome if time between X and the father did not go well, such as if X was detached and did not wish to engage, and that the father may have an idealised sense of who X is. He said, however that he would not expect a devastating or extreme reaction. He said he would expect that the father might experience disappointment and anger, but he did not think it likely that that anger would be directed towards X herself. He also said it was possible (though he did not consider it likely) that the father’s feelings would overwhelm him and he would be unable to engage and participate.
Dr P opined that the father was likely to find such a scenario very hard to deal with, and agreed that disengagement on his part was a possible outcome. He considered that the likely worst case scenario would be that the father would become agitated and irritated, and that it would make him more aggressive towards the mother.
Ms J gave evidence that if time were to commence but then the father disengaged, X would be likely to feel rejected and to feel that there was something about her that had caused the disengagement. X would then go from the current situation where her understanding of the lack of a relationship was external to her, to potentially internalising the causes of cessation of the relationship
Ms J also confirmed that there was a risk of inconsistency in presentation and in interaction by the father with X, which could compromise the development of their relationship and impact X’s sense of security.
I note also the father’s evidence that his reaction to not being permitted to spend time with X after she was born was to take illicit drugs to the point where he “lost [his] mind.” This would appear to be the best available evidence of the manner in which the father reacts if things do not go his way with respect to formation of his relationship with her.
Dr P, and to a lesser extent, Dr H, also agreed that there could be negative consequences arising from mistrust and suspicion of the mother on the father’s part were the parties to attempt to coparent.

Mother’s mental health and functioning

An important consideration in these proceedings is the mother’s mental health and functioning. The mother is X’s uncontested primary carer and it is not suggested that her mental health in and of itself poses a risk to X. Rather, the important issue for consideration by the Court is the extent to which an order for time or communication between X and the father could impact upon the mother’s mental health and impair her functioning as a parent and her ability to provide safe and secure parenting to X. It is well established that such impacts on the primary caregiver of a child can be a relevant consideration.[32]
The mother has a complex trauma history, which includes multiple experiences of violence and abuse.
